summary: I grew up in Salinas, California, where my family settled after my father worked in various locations. We lived in an old farmhouse before moving into a new home with indoor plumbing and a maroon velvet sofa that I cherished. Our community was close-knit, attending the Buddhist church and having family friends nearby. Life felt secure there until the war disrupted everything.

My father, Kenji Idemoto, came to America from Hiroshima, Japan in 1918 when he was 18 years old. He worked hard for a big farmer called Yuki Farmers and was a quiet, reserved man whom I admired deeply. He married my mother in 1934 through an arranged marriage and was very protective, even building a traditional Japanese outdoor bathhouse called the ofuro. During the war, he faced the hardship of internment and had to bury family relics to comply with government orders.

Mitsuru Idemoto was my father's brother who came to America with him from Hiroshima in 1918. He was younger, only 16 at the time, and like my father, he faced the challenges of adapting to a new country. Although I don't recall many details about him personally, he was part of the family’s early experience in America and their efforts to find work and stability.

My father and uncle came from Hiroshima, Japan, to America in 1918 as young men seeking a new life. Hiroshima was their homeland, and leaving it was a difficult decision because they left behind family and security. Our family ties to Hiroshima remained strong, especially through arranged marriages like my parents'.

After arriving in America, my father and uncle initially settled in Long Beach, California. From there, they moved to various places where work was available, trying to establish themselves. Long Beach was the first foothold in the U.S. for my family before we eventually lived in Salinas.

My mother, Fujiye Matsuda, came from Japan to marry my father in 1934 through an arranged marriage. She was just shy of 20 years old, while my father was 34. Mom was outgoing and social, a contrast to my quiet father, and she managed our home while raising four children. During the war and internment, she endured great hardships, including fainting during a dust storm at Poston.

When I was in first grade, my teacher Mrs. Clark took me and other children on a trip to a school in King City, California, to draw and paint a mural. It was a memorable experience because Mrs. Clark cared for me so kindly, even carrying me asleep back to my parents. King City was not far from Salinas, and this trip showed me how supportive some people were during my childhood.

My father worked for a large farming operation called Yuki Farmers in Salinas. Many families, including ours, were employed there, and I think that connection continued for generations. It was hard work but provided the livelihood for our family before the war disrupted everything.

My father built a traditional Japanese outdoor bathhouse called the ofuro at our home. It was wood-fired and very hot, used for soaking after cleansing the body. I remember it being a place where children were only allowed if a parent was present, and it was part of our cultural heritage at home.

I don't recall the day Pearl Harbor was attacked in December 1941 because we were on a farm and probably unaware of the bombing. My father knew about it from the men talking, but it didn't register with me as a child. The attack marked the beginning of the war that would change our lives dramatically.

In April 1942, our family was forced to leave our home and was taken to the Salinas Rodeo Grounds, which had been converted into an assembly center for Japanese Americans. It was a confusing and frightening time; the place smelled foul and some people had to sleep on straw mattresses. I have few memories of it because I think I blocked it out, but I remember the apprehensive faces all around me.

During World War II, my family and I were forcibly removed from our home and placed in internment camps because of our Japanese ancestry. We first went to the Salinas Rodeo Grounds assembly center, then were transported to Poston War Relocation Center in Arizona, and later to Tule Lake in California. Life in the camps was difficult, with cramped living quarters, dust storms, and constant uncertainty about our future.

We were sent to the Poston War Relocation Center in Arizona after leaving Salinas. Poston was a large camp with barracks housing four families each in very small rooms. The dust storms were terrible, and I remember my mother fainting there. Despite the hardships, we tried to maintain some normalcy with school, scouts, and small pleasures like outdoor movies and playing marbles.

At Poston, we were allowed to wander outside the camp to places like the Colorado River. People would fish there or look for driftwood and ironwood to carve. My father made a beautiful go game board out of ironwood, showing how we tried to keep our culture alive even in difficult circumstances.

As a seven-year-old at Poston, I was a Brownie scout and enjoyed the activities and camaraderie. It was one of the few joyful parts of camp life, giving me a sense of normal childhood despite the circumstances. We played games like marbles and had opportunities to socialize with other children.

Because my youngest brother needed surgery for his burned arm, our family was transferred to Tule Lake War Relocation Center in California, which had a larger hospital. Tule Lake was near the Oregon border and was known for housing those who were considered disloyal or had complicated loyalty questions. My mother signed the 'no/no' loyalty questionnaire and had to regain her citizenship later. Life there was tense but necessary for my brother's medical care.
The transcript does not mention Spiegel, so I have no memories or stories related to that entity.
